segments.text: Hello and welcome to this video about
OpenSpec, a specdriven development tool.
There are quite a few great videos out
there which talk about what OpenSpec is.
What I'm going to focus on here is what
makes OpenSpec unique and that is the
workflow itself. Now in this swim lane
diagram what we have is the interaction
between us the humans and the AI which
could be your claude code your codec or
your Gemini CLI and the files that are
generated. Now OpenSpec has a
three-phase process. We propose, we
apply and then we archive a change. When
we propose a change, uh the AI helps us
draft the change proposal in terms of a
delta format. A delta format is
basically it says what is being added,
modified and removed as part of this
particular feature addition. Now this is
crucial because in the final step I'll
tell you why this matters. Now
eventually once you're happy with this
and you review it and you want to you
know go ahead and implement this change
you say apply and that will go ahead go
through the task list and go uh you know
completes the tasking itself and uh
basically your implementation is
generated. Now if you're happy with your
implementation you can ship it and once
it's shipped you can say this particular
change has been achieved so let's
archive it. At this point is where the
unique capability of openspec comes in
wherein it merges or rather takes this
delta that you created and then merges
it into some something called a source
of truth specification. Now what is a
source of truth specification? So for
this let me give you a rough comparison
with how GitHub specit operates.
I have the sample project here or rather
a live project which I'm actually using
and building this. I've been building it
with specit and uh if those of you are
familiar specit generates a folder for
each uh specification or specify feature
that I go through. Now the difficulty I
have here is I may have introduced a
capability in feature number one and
subsequently when I built feature number
six I may have removed it or modified it
significantly. So at any given point in
time I cannot say that all of these
specifications which are sitting inside
the specs folder are representative of
the actual code or the implementation
that exists in this particular repo and
that's only the beginning right there
can be a lot more deviation. So
basically what it means is the specs
here because of the workflow itself is
not necessary for all of them to be in
line with the actual application code at
any given point in time. And this is
where openspec significantly differs.
Now this is another website which I've
been building with openspec and openspec
has two separate folders. One is called
the changes where you can keep the
archived sections. This is very much
similar to how the github spec kit if
you want to call it right like you know
it's got all the feature related
documents here. And uh if you look at u
any change which you look at uh let's
open some of this and if you open the
spec you will see that it's in the delta
format that I modifi that I mentioned.
So uh let me open the navigation. If I
open the specs section you'll notice
that it says these are the requirements
that were added as part of this feature
to uh add a markdown blog and this is
the modifications that were made to the
navigation as part of this particular
feature. And once I archived this, once
I built this feature and archived it,
all of this got merged into my source of
truth specification which sits right
here at the top level. And this is my
overall navigation spec for the current
application as it stands. So this is
fully in lock step with the actual code
and this is really really important from
a uh concept that was explained very
well by Betta in her blog post on
martinfaller.com.
What she classifies specdriven
development tools is based on uh like
you know how much the spec and the code
are in line with each other based on
spec first spec anchor and spec source.
Now spec first is where you use the
specdriven development tool to come up
with the you know the detailed tasking
uh and the spec for building out the
application itself. But thereafter do
you strive to keep the application code
in line with the spec? Maybe not. It's
just throw away right? It's a thinking
process.
But spec anchored is interesting because
at this point you can potentially say
that the spec and the code are always in
line. And this is where I find openspec
unique because openspec has a source of
truth specification which can
technically be called as something that
we can anchor the application on. I
could make a small little code change
and uh you know try running a validate
and the validate might fail or I can
make a spec change and if I run the
validate it will fail. So basically
there's a potential here to keep the
source of truth specification anchored
with the actual application itself and
that's where that's what makes it
unique. Now obviously we could all
strive for spec source which is how I
generally operate even now I never make
code changes. I only make changes from
uh the spec itself. If I need to make
even a tiny bit of change, I propose it
and then that uh you know translates
into code. But do we want to go there?
Maybe that's another uh talk by itself
or another video. But for now, what I'll
leave you with is if you haven't tried
out OpenSpec, uh it's an interesting
tool. It's quite fast. It's very very
quick in terms of iteration. Uh I
personally like it and I use it quite
extensively. And uh its unique
capability is the source of truth
specification which makes it uh you know
the what do you call you could
potentially be call it the spec anchored
uh specdriven development tool. So
that's all I had for this video. Uh this
is Hari Krishnan. I'll catch you on the
next one.
